docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
MySQL is a relational database management system used to store, organize, and manage data. It allows users to create databases, store information in tables, and run queries to retrieve or modify data.

This documentation explains how to use MySQL Workbench on Windows to do basic database work with its GUI (graphical interface). It will walk you through creating a connection, creating a schema (database), creating tables, running queries, and viewing or editing data in Workbench.

This is a beginner's guide to Workbench’s GUI, not a detailed guide to database theory or advanced MySQL.

docs.title: Intended Users
docs.text:
This documentation is designed for users who want to learn how to use MySQL Workbench for basic database tasks.

  • Beginner developers who want to learn how to install and use MySQL.
  • Students learning database management and core MySQL functionalities.
  • First-time users who have never worked with a database GUI before.

docs.title: Prerequisite Knowledge
docs.text: Before following this guide, users should be familiar with basic computer usage. This includes opening and navigating applications, downloading and installing software, and using a mouse and keyboard to interact with system interfaces. Users should also be comfortable navigating files and folders within the Windows operating system.

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text:
This guide assumes that the following software is already correctly installed on your Windows computer. This is written specifically for people running the Windows OS.

  • MySQL Server
  • MySQL Workbench

docs.title: Typographical Conventions
docs.text: Convention Explanation Examples Commands & actions Bolded words for commands or actions you need to perform. Click, Enter, Type, Return, Open, Select Code, SQL, and file names Monospace text for SQL commands. SELECT * FROM users; Important terms Important terms are underlined when they are first introduced. Hovering over them shows their definition. schema, table, query

docs.title: Notes, Warnings, and Results
docs.text:
You'll see branded callouts throughout the guide to highlight important information.

Warning

Steps marked Warning explain prerequisites or important conditions that may prevent you from completing the tutorial successfully.

Note

Note boxes provide extra explanation about what MySQL or Workbench is doing and why a step matters.

Success

Success shows the expected result so you can confirm you’re on the right track.

docs.title: Create a connection
docs.text: Before creating schemas, tables, or queries in MySQL Workbench, you first need to connect to an SQL Server. This part shows how to create a new connection in Workbench.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open MySQL Workbench.

  2. On the home screen, click the + icon next to MySQL Connections.

  3. In the Setup New Connection window, enter these connection details:

    • Connection Name: enter a name for the connection
    • Hostname: localhost
    • Port: 3306
    • Username: root

  4. Click Test Connection to verify that the connection settings are correct. If prompted, enter your MySQL password.

Success

If the connection settings are correct, Workbench will display a successful connection message:

  1. Click OK to save the connection.

Note

If the connection test fails, double-check your password, hostname, and port number (3306). Most connection issues are caused by incorrect login details.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You've created a connection in MySQL Workbench. You can use this to access your MySQL server to do things like creating a schema, creating tables, and running queries.

docs.title: Create a Schema
docs.text:
A schema in MySQL Workbench represents a database that stores tables and data in those tables.

This section explains how to create a new schema using the MySQL Workbench graphical interface.

Warning

Make sure you already created a connection, and you opened the connection. Refer to Create a connection.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. In MySQL Workbench, locate the schema panel on the left side of the menu interface.

  2. Click the Create Schema button.

  3. In the Schema Name field, enter the name of your new schema.

  4. Click Apply to begin creating the schema.

  5. A confirmation window will appear showing the SQL command was executed.

  6. Click Finish.

  7. Verify that the schema appears in the SCHEMAS section.

Note

Use clear and simple names for your schema (e.g., school_db or inventory). Avoid spaces or special characters, as they may cause issues when writing SQL queries.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You've created a schema in MySQL Workbench. Your schema is ready to store tables and data now.

docs.title: Create a table
docs.text:
An SQL table stores data in rows and columns. Columns define the kind of data each entry can hold, and rows contain the actual data. This section shows how to create a table using Workbench.

Warning

Before making a table, make sure you have already created a schema. Refer to Create a schema.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. In MySQL Workbench, locate the schema panel on the left side.

  2. Expand your schema by clicking the arrow next to its name.

  3. Right-click on the Tables section inside your schema.

  4. Click Create Table.

  5. In the Table Name field, enter a name for your table.

  6. In the Columns section, define your table structure by adding columns one at a time.

    • Enter a column name.

    • Select a data type in the Datatype field, such as INT, VARCHAR, or DATE.

    • Check any needed options, such as PK for primary key, NN for not null, or AI for auto increment.

  7. Click Apply

  8. A confirmation window will appear showing that the table has been created.

  9. Click Apply again, then click Finish

  10. Verify that your table has been created by navigating back to your tables section in your schema.

Note

It is recommended to create a Primary Key (PK) for your table, often using an INT column with Auto Increment (AI). This ensures each row has a unique identifier.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You've created a table in Workbench. Now you can start to inserting data into it.

docs.title: Insert data
docs.text:
In MySQL Workbench, data is stored inside tables as rows. To add new information, users insert data into the columns of a table. This section explains how to insert data using the MySQL Workbench graphical interface.

Warning

Before inserting data, make sure you have already created a table. Refer to Create a table.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. In MySQL Workbench, locate the schemas panel on the left side.

  2. Expand your schema and locate the table you want to insert data into.

  3. Right-click the table and click Select Rows.

  4. A table view will open showing the current rows.

  5. Click on an empty row (usually marked with NULL ) at the bottom of the table.

  6. Enter values into each column.

    • Type directly into each cell.

    • Follow the datatype requirements (e.g. numbers for INT, text for VARCHAR).

    • If a column is set to Auto Increment, you can leave it blank and it will be filled automatically.

  7. Click Apply in the bottom right corner.

    • If you accidentally entered incorrect data, you can click Revert beside Apply to erase what you just entered.

  8. A confirmation window will appear showing the SQL statement used to insert the data.

  9. Click Apply, then click Finish.

  10. Verify that your row appears in the table, by clicking Refresh.

Note

Make sure required columns ( marked with NN for Not Null ) are filled in before clicking Apply, or the insert may fail.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Now your table contains saved rows, and you can now view, edit, or query that data. We'll query that data in the next part.

docs.title: Running queries
docs.text:
A query is an SQL command used to retrieve or work with data. In MySQL Workbench, queries are written and run in the SQL Editor. This part explains how to run a query using Workbench.

Warning

Before following part, make sure you've already inserted data into a table, and you have your connection opened. Refer to Inserting data into tables.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. In MySQL Workbench, locate the schemas panel on the left side.

  2. Click the button circled in red to expand the schema, and then expand "Tables" to confirm that your table exists.

  3. Click File and click New Query Tab to create a new query tab in the SQL Editor. You can also press Ctrl + T to create a tab.

    The area highlighted in red is your SQL Editor. This is where you write and run queries:

  4. In the new tab in the SQL Editor type:

    USE name_of_your_schema;
SELECT * FROM name_of_your_table;

    Replace the placeholder names with the actual names of your schema and table.

    Note

    This part uses a USE and SELECT query because it's safe for beginners.

  5. Click the Execute button (lightning bolt icon) to run the query.

    Success

    If the query runs successfully, you will see the data from your table in the Result Grid, as well as an Output showing the query that was executed.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You just ran a query in Workbench. You can use the SQL Editor to view data, check results, and work with your schema is basic SQL commands.

docs.title: View and edit data
docs.text:
In MySQL Workbench you can view and change data in a table, allowing you to update values, correct mistakes, and more. This part shows how to view and edit table data in Workbench.

Warning

Before following part, make sure you've already inserted data into a table, and you have your connection opened. Refer to Inserting data into tables.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. In MySQL Workbench, locate the schemas panel on the left side.

  2. Expand your schema and locate the table you want to view or edit.

  3. Right-click the table you want to edit, and left-click Select Rows.

  4. A table view will open showing the current data.

  5. To edit a value, click directly on the cell you want to change and type the new value.

  6. To delete a row, right-click the row on the left, then select Delete Row

  7. Click Apply in the bottom right corner to save your changes

  8. A confirmation window will appear showing the SQL statement used to update or delete the data.

  9. Click Apply, then click Finish.

    Success

    You have successfully updated your data in your table.

  10. Verify that your changes have been applied by clicking Refresh.

Note

Be careful when editing values in a Primary Key column. Primary Keys must remain unique, so changing them may cause errors or affect how rows are identified.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You just viewed and edited table data in Workbench. This way you can update existing values and remove unwanted rows.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Possible Cause Solution Cannot click the AI check box. Column datatype is not numeric or not set as Primary Key. Change datatype to INT and enable PK, then select AI. Error when inserting text into a column. Incorrect datatype (e.g. using INT instead of VARCHAR). Change the column datatype to VARCHAR for text values and INT for numeric values. Table does not appear after creation. Schema not refreshed. Right click the schema and click Refresh. Cannot type in a cell when inserting data. Table is not in edit mode. Click on an empty row (NULL row) at the bottom to start editing. Cannot find the table when inserting data. Wrong schema selected. Make sure you are using the correct schema. Text is being cut off or truncated. VARCHAR length is too short. Increase the size (e.g. change VARCHAR(5) to VARCHAR(50)). Cannot delete a row. Changes not applied or wrong row selected. Select the correct row, then click Apply after deleting. Error about duplicate values. Primary Key must be unique. Enter a unique value or avoid changing the Primary Key. Query returns an error message. Syntax error in SQL. Check for missing keywords, commas, or semicolons in your query. Table does not exist error. Incorrect table name. Make sure the table name matches exactly, including spelling. Unknown database error. Schema name is incorrect or not selected. Verify the schema name and include the USE command before running the query. Cannot connect to the MySQL server. The MySQL server is not running, or the connection settings are incorrect. Make sure the MySQL server is running, then check the hostname, port, username, and password in your connection settings. Access denied for user 'root'. The password is incorrect, or the account does not have permission to connect. Re-enter the correct password and make sure you are using the correct MySQL account. No database selected appears when running a query. No default schema is selected in MySQL Workbench. Double-click your schema in the SCHEMAS panel to set it as the default schema, then run the query again. The query runs, but no rows appear in the Result Grid. The table is empty, or no matching data has been inserted yet. Make sure you inserted data into the table first, then run the query again. Changes disappear after editing data. The changes were not applied. After editing the row, click Apply and complete the confirmation step to save the changes.
